criteria: A sharply scoped tool allow-list (self-contained, minimal overlap with sibling subagents) and a description precise enough that the orchestrator reliably delegates to it and not another subagent — the structured-prompting checklist applies to the system prompt, but the frontmatter contract (name/description/tools/model) is validated separately.

entries.label: good
entries.content:
---
name: code-reviewer
description: Reviews a pull request diff for correctness bugs, security issues, and reuse opportunities. Use PROACTIVELY after any non-trivial code change is ready for review — not for style/lint issues, which a linter already catches, and not for architecture-level design review, which belongs to a design-review subagent.
tools: Read, Grep, Glob
model: inherit
---
You are a senior code reviewer...
entries.rationale: Description states exactly what this subagent does (correctness/security/reuse review of a diff), when to invoke it (proactively, post-change), and explicitly what it does NOT do (style/lint, architecture review) with a pointer to the sibling subagent that owns that instead — minimizing delegation overlap. Tool allow-list is minimal and read-only, matching a review-only role (no Write/Edit — it reports, it doesn't fix).

entries.label: good
entries.content:
---
name: test-runner
description: Runs the project's test suite and reports pass/fail counts plus the first 3 failure stack traces. Use when asked to "run the tests", "check if tests pass", or after a code change that could affect test outcomes. Does not fix failing tests — that's the calling agent's job with this subagent's output as input.
tools: Bash
model: haiku
---
You are a test-execution subagent...
entries.rationale: Narrow, single-purpose description with explicit trigger phrases an orchestrator can pattern-match against, and an explicit non-goal ("does not fix failing tests") that prevents mis-delegation into fix-it territory. Tool allow-list is exactly what's needed (Bash only) and no more. Cheap model choice matches the mechanical nature of the task.

entries.label: bad
entries.content:
---
name: helper
description: Helps with various coding and analysis tasks.
tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebFetch, WebSearch
model: opus
---
You are a helpful assistant that can do many things...
entries.rationale: "Helps with various coding and analysis tasks" gives an orchestrator no signal for when to delegate here versus any other subagent — maximal mis-delegation risk. Tool allow-list is essentially everything, the opposite of a sharply scoped subagent. No explicit non-goals or boundaries stated anywhere.

entries.label: bad
entries.content:
---
name: quality-checker
description: Checks code quality, reviews changes, and can also run tests if needed.
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Edit
---
You check quality and sometimes fix things...
entries.rationale: Description overlaps heavily with both a code-reviewer subagent ("reviews changes") and a test-runner subagent ("can also run tests") with no stated boundary between them — an orchestrator has no reliable signal for which subagent to pick, exactly the mis-delegation risk the doc calls out. "Sometimes fix things" in the body contradicts a review-only tool allow-list expectation, and Edit access on a nominally read-only quality checker is scope creep.
